sourceFiles.name: thermal-conflict

fix.title: Make shutdown threshold a required attribute and bind the design value to the requirement
fix.description: This fix removes the unlinked duplication by keeping the EmergencyShutdown requirement as the normative source of intent while making the threshold explicit as a requirement attribute. The CoolingSystem design attribute is then bound to that requirement attribute through a satisfy relationship and a binding constraint inside a requirement usage context. This creates direct traceability and prevents the requirement text and implementation threshold from diverging silently.

changes.description: Updated the EmergencyShutdown requirement to define the shutdown threshold as an explicit requirement attribute and reference it in the requirement text, making the requirement the authoritative source for the threshold value.

Added a requirement usage and a satisfy relationship with an explicit bind so the CoolingSystem design attribute is traceably constrained to the requirement threshold rather than duplicating it without linkage.

fix.title: Make cooling fan threshold attribute the authoritative source and bind the requirement to it
fix.description: This fix removes the duplicated numeric threshold from the CoolingFanActivation requirement text and introduces an explicit constraint on the design attribute inside the requirement, making thermal_system::CoolingSystem::coolingFanStartThresholdC the single authoritative threshold definition. The requirement now traces directly to the structural attribute value instead of restating it in prose, eliminating divergence risk while preserving the requirement intent.

changes.description: Reworded the CoolingFanActivation requirement to remove the duplicated literal threshold from prose and added a requirement attribute plus constraint so the threshold can be explicitly bound to the design value.

Added an explicit satisfy usage in CoolingSystem that binds the requirement threshold parameter to coolingFanStartThresholdC, making the structural attribute the authoritative source and providing traceability.

fix.title: Make SprinklerActivation the authoritative threshold and remove duplicated attribute default
fix.description: This fix resolves the redundancy by keeping the 90 degrees Celsius trigger only in the requirement and removing the repeated numeric default from SprinklerSystem. The model now has a single authoritative source for the sprinkler activation threshold, eliminating maintenance risk from duplicated threshold values.

changes.description: Removed the duplicated default value from SprinklerSystem.activationThresholdC so the sprinkler activation temperature is specified only by the SprinklerActivation requirement.

fix.title: Reorder thermal response thresholds so staged mitigations activate before shutdown
fix.description: This fix removes the unreachable behavior by changing the operating semantics to a staged thermal response: the cooling fan activates first, then the sprinkler system, and emergency shutdown occurs last. The requirement texts and threshold attributes are updated consistently so fan and sprinkler activation are reachable before shutdown, eliminating the redundancy identified in the review finding.

changes.description: Updated the thermal response thresholds in both requirements and attributes to a reachable staged order: cooling fan at 80C, sprinkler at 85C, and emergency shutdown at 90C.

results.name: 04_redundancy
results.rawText:
Model Characterisation
- System purpose: A thermal protection/control model defining when a system should shut down, start cooling, and activate sprinklers based on temperature thresholds.
- Operational domain: Industrial / safety-related thermal management.
- Model scope: Small model fragment containing requirement definitions and structural part definitions with threshold attributes.
- Known scope limitations: No behaviours, state machines, actions, verification cases, relationships (`satisfy`, `allocate`, `refine`, `deriveReqt`), packages beyond one namespace, or parametrics are included. Checks depending on those artefacts are assessed only for explicit absence and otherwise skipped with rationale.

CHECK PASSED: 1. Duplicate Requirements  -  The three requirement definitions have distinct texts and distinct threshold values (80°C, 85°C, 90°C); no identical or near-identical requirement duplication is present in the provided fragment.

CHECK PASSED: 2. Duplicate Block or Part Definitions  -  The model contains three distinct part definitions (`CoolingSystem`, `SprinklerSystem`, `System`) with different roles and content; no duplicated block/interface definitions are present.

CHECK PASSED: 3. Redundant Relationships  -  No explicit `satisfy`, `allocate`, `connect`, `bind`, or specialization relationships are present, so no duplicated relationship statements can be identified in this fragment.

CHECK PASSED: 4. Redundant Behaviour Models  -  No state machines, action definitions, sequences, or message exchange models are present, so there is no duplicated behavioural model content to assess.

ISSUE [REDUNDANCY-001]
Element(s):     thermal_system::EmergencyShutdown; thermal_system::CoolingSystem::emergencyShutdownThresholdC
Check:          5. Redundant Attributes & Constraints
Finding:        The emergency shutdown threshold is defined twice: once in the requirement text and again as a numeric attribute default in the design definition, without any explicit derivation, binding, satisfy, or trace relationship connecting them.
Evidence:       Requirement text: `requirement def EmergencyShutdown { doc /* The system shall enter emergency shutdown when temperature reaches 80 degrees Celsius. */ }`; Attribute: `attribute emergencyShutdownThresholdC : Real = 80.0;`
Score:          0.72  -  Minor-to-moderate duplication creates maintenance risk because requirement and design threshold can diverge silently.
Confidence:     HIGH
Risk:           If one threshold is updated and the other is not, the model may simultaneously claim conflicting shutdown behaviour in requirements versus structure, undermining analysis and verification consistency.
Recommendation: Keep the requirement as the normative source for intent, and either derive/bind the design threshold explicitly from that requirement or annotate the attribute as the implementation of the requirement with traceability.

ISSUE [REDUNDANCY-002]
Element(s):     thermal_system::CoolingFanActivation; thermal_system::CoolingSystem::coolingFanStartThresholdC
Check:          5. Redundant Attributes & Constraints
Finding:        The cooling fan activation threshold is duplicated between requirement text and a structural attribute value, with no explicit reuse or traceability mechanism showing that one is derived from the other.
Evidence:       Requirement text: `requirement def CoolingFanActivation { doc /* The cooling fan shall turn on when temperature reaches 85 degrees Celsius. */ }`; Attribute: `attribute coolingFanStartThresholdC : Real = 85.0;`
Score:          0.72  -  Minor-to-moderate duplication increases divergence risk between requirement wording and implementation data.
Confidence:     HIGH
Risk:           Future changes could leave the requirement and the modelled threshold inconsistent, causing incorrect verification expectations or design reviews based on stale values.
Recommendation: Retain a single authoritative threshold definition where possible; otherwise add explicit traceability or a constraint/redefinition mechanism linking the attribute value to the requirement.

ISSUE [REDUNDANCY-003]
Element(s):     thermal_system::SprinklerActivation; thermal_system::SprinklerSystem::activationThresholdC
Check:          5. Redundant Attributes & Constraints
Finding:        The sprinkler activation threshold is stated in both a requirement and an attribute default, again without any explicit derivation, binding, or satisfaction relation to justify the repeated numeric definition.
Evidence:       Requirement text: `requirement def SprinklerActivation { doc /* The sprinkler system shall turn on when temperature reaches 90 degrees Celsius. */ }`; Attribute: `attribute activationThresholdC : Real = 90.0;`
Score:          0.72  -  Minor-to-moderate duplicated specification of the same numeric trigger creates avoidable maintenance burden.
Confidence:     HIGH
Risk:           Threshold mismatches can emerge over time, leading to inconsistent hazard analysis, verification criteria, or implementation assumptions.
Recommendation: Merge the duplicated threshold semantics by making one source authoritative and linking the other through explicit traceability or formal derivation.

CHECK PASSED: 6. Redundant Decomposition  -  The decomposition is minimal and purposeful (`System` contains `cooling` and `sprinkler`); there are no empty packages, pass-through blocks, or single-child action decompositions in the provided model.

CHECK PASSED: 7. Unintentional vs. Intentional Redundancy  -  No duplicated architectural elements suggesting deliberate fault-tolerance redundancy are present; the identified threshold repetitions are specification-level duplication rather than intentional design redundancy.

ISSUE [REDUNDANCY-004]
Element(s):     thermal_system::EmergencyShutdown; thermal_system::CoolingFanActivation; thermal_system::SprinklerActivation
Check:          8. States that are never activated
Finding:        The activation conditions for the cooling fan (85°C) and sprinkler system (90°C) are rendered unreachable by the emergency shutdown condition at 80°C. These higher-threshold responses are therefore redundant in the current model logic.
Evidence:       `EmergencyShutdown`: `The system shall enter emergency shutdown when temperature reaches 80 degrees Celsius.`; `CoolingFanActivation`: `The cooling fan shall turn on when temperature reaches 85 degrees Celsius.`; `SprinklerActivation`: `The sprinkler system shall turn on when temperature reaches 90 degrees Celsius.`
Score:          0.35  -  Significant defect because the modelled threshold scheme makes later responses unusable, likely leading to incorrect behavioural interpretation.
Confidence:     HIGH
Risk:           Analysts may believe the system includes staged thermal mitigation, when in fact shutdown precludes fan and sprinkler activation; this can invalidate safety reasoning, verification planning, and response coverage analysis.
Recommendation: Rework the thresholds or operating semantics so that fan/sprinkler activation can occur before shutdown, or explicitly state that these functions operate in a different mode or independently of shutdown if that is the intended behaviour.

Summary Table

| ID | Score | Confidence | One-line description |
|---|---:|---|---|
| REDUNDANCY-001 | 0.72 | HIGH | Emergency shutdown threshold is duplicated in requirement text and an attribute value. |
| REDUNDANCY-002 | 0.72 | HIGH | Cooling fan start threshold is duplicated in requirement text and an attribute value. |
| REDUNDANCY-003 | 0.72 | HIGH | Sprinkler activation threshold is duplicated in requirement text and an attribute value. |
| REDUNDANCY-004 | 0.35 | HIGH | Fan and sprinkler activations are redundant because shutdown occurs first and makes them unreachable. |

Overall Assessment Score: 0.63
Model Status: Significant issues - not suitable for baseline or verification activities
